title: Will the Federal Reserve Hike rates by 0bps at their July 2026 meeting?

current.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 91%, modal YES) — staying out.
current.rationale_summary: The evidence points to a high likelihood of the Federal Reserve holding rates steady in July 2026, with odds from the source indicating around 80.5% to 81.5% for no rate hike. This aligns with a reference class of previous Fed meetings where rate hikes were less likely, and the current evidence suggests a continuation of this trend. While there is still some uncertainty, the data supports a high p
key_drivers: historical Fed behavior in similar economic contexts
reference_classes: previous Fed rate decisions in periods of economic stability
rate-hike probabilities from similar market forecasts

adversarial_concerns: Divergence from liquid market implies unexplained edge; market folds path risk into one meeting but forecaster assumes persistence without addressing Fed's explicit patience signal
Base-rate of 85% persistence after hold is cited but not reconciled with market's 74.5% (which may include delayed action risk)
Overconfidence in 0.8 vs market 0.745 without credible justification for tighter distribution
history.rationale_summary: June was a UNANIMOUS hold; next-meeting persistence after a unanimous hold is ~85%. The 9/8 dot split is full-year, not July-specific (any hike can land later). Persistent 3.6% PCE removes cut risk but Fed signaled patience. ~0.80, slightly above market 0.745 (which folds path risk into one meeting).
key_drivers: unanimous June hold -> high next-meeting persistence
dot split is full-year not July
PCE persistence biases alt to hike not cut
reference_classes: FOMC outcome after a unanimous hold
per-meeting realization of a year-level dot move

history.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 78%, modal YES) — staying out.
history.rationale_summary: The base rate for a 0bps rate hike by the Federal Reserve during a meeting is historically low, as the Fed typically adjusts rates in response to economic conditions. However, in a scenario where inflation is under control, unemployment is stable, and economic growth is moderate, the Fed may opt for a pause.
